problem_addressed: Standard benchmarks cannot measure how close neural networks are to optimal performance because they lack access to the true posterior p(y|x), making it impossible to distinguish irreducible aleatoric uncertainty from reducible epistemic error.
core_claim: Access to exact posteriors via class-conditional normalizing flows reveals hidden learning dynamics, architectural differences, and enables precise quantification of model limitations and data efficiency.
key_contributions: Demonstrates that epistemic error follows power-law scaling even when total loss plateaus, revealing ongoing learning beyond standard metrics.
Shows architectures differ markedly in approaching the aleatoric floor, with ResNets exhibiting clean power-law scaling while ViTs stall in low-data regimes.
Proves oracle posteriors contain learnable structure beyond class labels, enabling near-perfect calibration when used as soft labels.
Establishes that distribution shift type matters more than magnitude, as class imbalance causes minimal accuracy loss at KL divergences where input noise causes catastrophic degradation.
Shows exact epistemic uncertainty distinguishes informative samples from ambiguous ones, improving active learning sample efficiency.
novelty: First to make exact posteriors tractable on realistic images (AFHQ, ImageNet) using class-conditional normalizing flows, enabling decomposition of prediction error and precise measurement of aleatoric floors.
method_summary: Uses class-conditional normalizing flows as oracles to compute exact posteriors p(y|x) for realistic images, enabling decomposition of prediction error into aleatoric and epistemic components and exact KL divergence computation for distribution shift analysis.
method_components: Class-conditional normalizing flow oracles that make exact posteriors tractable
Decomposition of prediction error into irreducible aleatoric uncertainty and reducible epistemic error
Power-law fitting to quantify epistemic error scaling with dataset size
Exact KL divergence computation for controlled distribution shift perturbations
key_innovation: Exact posteriors enable precise decomposition of neural network error into aleatoric and epistemic components, revealing hidden learning dynamics and architectural differences invisible to standard metrics.
assumptions: Class-conditional normalizing flows can perfectly model true posteriors
Datasets (AFHQ, ImageNet) are representative of real-world image distributions
Power-law scaling holds across architectures and datasets
theoretical_grounding: Epistemic error follows power-law scaling N^−α (with α varying by architecture), aleatoric floor equals Bayes error, and exact KL divergence quantifies distribution shift magnitude and type.
headline_result: Epistemic error follows a power law decay (KL ∝ N^{-α}) across architectures and scales to ImageNet-1000 classes
improvement_over_baseline: ResNets reduce epistemic error by 6× over the data range where ViTs improve by only 1.2×
best_result: Epistemic error follows a power law decay (KL ∝ N^{-α}) across architectures and scales to ImageNet-1000 classes
title: Beyond the Loss Curve: Scaling Laws, Active Learning, and the Limits of Learning from Exact Posteriors
